alerts.title: Permanent fire prohibition on Teewah Beach
alerts.description: A QPWS imposed fire prohibition is in place in the Cooloola Recreation Area, except at Poverty Point camping area. Camp fires must not be lit anywhere else in the recreation area including in the Teewah Beach camping zones 1-7.
alerts.details:
A QPWS imposed fire prohibition is in place in the Cooloola Recreation Area, except at Poverty Point camping area. Camp fires must not be lit anywhere else in the recreation area including in the Teewah Beach camping zones 1-7.

This permanent fire prohibition was implemented to reduce the risk of bushfires, protect critical habitat of native wildlife, enhance visitor safety from camp fire related injuries and reduce rubbish from non-combustible materials being burnt.

Fully enclosed cooking and heating appliances can still be used in camping areas that meet the below requirements.

  • The appliance must use gas or manufactured fuel (heat beads, briquettes). Manufactured fuel does not include timber or wood.
  • It must be used only for cooking and/or heating.
  • It must be a minimum of 20cm off the ground and more than 2m from flammable materials, which includes vegetation. 
  • It must not have the potential to generate airborne embers, or possibly ignite nearby ground fuels.
  • It must be fully self-contained (heat source enclosed and no exposed flame) and never left unattended when in use.
  • Appliances that are not fully self-contained, such as braziers, are not permitted.
  • All appliances, materials and burnt fuel must be removed from the recreation area on your departure.

On-the-spot fines of $863 can be issued to people who light fires during a fire prohibition.

Camp fires are permitted at Poverty Point camping area and at nearby Inskip Peninsula Recreation Area unless as fire ban or prohibition is in place. Always check local directions and signs before lighting a fire. 

For details on the difference between a Queensland Fire Department (QFD) total fire ban and a QPWS fire prohibition and approved cooking appliances visit the DETSI Camp fires and cooking web page.

 

Please monitor park alerts on the department's website for updated information.

Stay up-to-date with Current bushfire warnings and incidents from Queensland Fire Department (QFD).

alerts.title: George Point toilet and sign removed due to erosion
alerts.description: George Point camping area on the southern coastline of Hinchinbrook Island National Park has been affected by severe erosion due to ongoing high tides over the past several years.
alerts.details:
George Point camping area on the southern coastline of Hinchinbrook Island National Park has been affected by severe erosion due to ongoing high tides over the past several years.

The camping area signage and infrastructure, including the toilet, have been removed. There is currently no signage to identify the pickup point for hikers.

There is bush camping available nearby if needed by Thorsborne Trail hikers or sea kayakers. Space for camping is available under a large stand of trees approximately 2.6km south of the Mulligan Creek crossing along the beach (prior to reaching the old camping area when hiking from north to south). Look out for a sign identifying the new camping area. Please be aware there is no toilet/pack rack. There is no boat-based camping available.

The George Point pickup point is 250m southwest of the bush camping space, along the beach.

QPWS is currently reviewing options for the future of the site as part of the Thorsborne Trail Enhancement project.

alerts.title: Camp site booking restrictions due to tidal inundation
alerts.description: Several low-lying camp sites adjacent to Pumicestone Passage on the western side of Bribie Island Recreation Area will be closed on a recurring basis throughout 2026 due to tidal inundation from forecast large high tides.
alerts.details:
Several low-lying camp sites adjacent to Pumicestone Passage on the western side of Bribie Island Recreation Area will be closed on a recurring basis throughout 2026 due to tidal inundation from forecast large high tides.

Affected camp sites include:

  • Lime Pocket camping area - All sites
  • Mission Point camping area - Sites 1-6 
  • Gallagher Point camping area - All sites
  • Poverty Creek camping area - Sites 4, 5 & 6

Please note that tide times and heights are taken from the Brisbane Bar tide chart. This is due to the changes in water flow caused by the northern breakaway section of Bribie Island.

On waterfront sites, campers should, where possible, consider placing tents and camping equipment back from the water’s edge to avoid any inundation from passing boats.

Please observe all signage, barriers and directions from rangers and do not enter closed areas.

Stay up to date with weather forecasts, conditions and warnings.

alerts.details:
Please be advised that access to the Diggings Camping Area is currently impacted due to deteriorating road conditions on Diggings/Hazelwood Road . These conditions have arisen as a result of Cyclone Koji and ongoing adverse weather events in the region.

Sections of Diggings/Hazelwood Road have significant road ruts and potholes, exacerbated by significant rainfall experienced during recent weather events.  

For your safety, we strongly recommend exercising caution when travelling in the area. Visitors are encouraged to:
-  Check road conditions before travelling.
-  Avoid unnecessary travel if conditions worsen.
-  Use suitable vehicles for unsealed and challenging road conditions.
-  Follow all signage and directions from local authorities.

QPWS is working with the owners of the road towards a solution to restore suitable access to the Diggings Camping Area.

For your safety please proceed with caution along all park access roads.

alerts.title: Severe Weather Damage - Lamington National Park
alerts.description: As a result of damage from Tropical Cyclone Alfred, some areas in Lamington National Park are closed until declared safe.
alerts.details:
As a result of damage from Tropical Cyclone Alfred, some areas in Lamington National Park are closed until declared safe.

QPWS Rangers are managing the situation and have damage assessment and repair activities in progress.

CLOSED Areas:

Binna Burra section

  • Illinbah circuit (eastern side) - Closed from the first junction on the Gwongoorool track to the Illinbah bush camp. The western side of the Illinbah circuit remains open (access is from the second junction on the Gwongoorool track).  Once visitors reach the Illinbah bush camp, please return via the same track.
  • Coomera circuit - Partial closure from the Coomera Falls lookout to the southern junction on the Border track (second turn-off) - The only access to the Coomera Falls lookout is from the northern junction on the Border track (first turn-off) - once visitors reach the Coomera Falls lookout, please return via the same track.

Green Mountains section

  • Albert River circuit - Partial closure from the northern junction on the Border track (first turn-off) to Echo Point - The only access to Echo Point is from the southern junction on the Border track (second turn-off).  Once visitors reach Echo Point, please return via the same track.

OPEN Areas:

  • Gold Coast Hinterland Great Walk track.
  • Border track (between Binna Burra and Green Mountains/O'Reilly's)

Binna Burra section

  • Upper Day-use Area and trailhead visitor facilities, including toilets
  • Rainforest circuit
  • Tullawallal circuit
  • Caves track
  • Gwongoorool track
  • Daves Creek circuit
  • Lower Bellbird track
  • Wagawn track
  • Araucaria track
  • Mount Hobwee circuit
  • Ship Stern circuit
  • Coomera circuit - Partially open from the northern junction on the Border track (first turn-off) to the Coomera Falls lookout only - once visitors reach the Coomera Falls lookout, please return via the same track.
  • Upper Ballanjui Falls (please return via the same track)
  • Illinbah circuit (western side) - Open from the second junction on the Gwongoorool track to the Illinbah bush camp - Once visitors reach the Illinbah bush camp - please return via the same track. The eastern side will remain temporarily closed until declared safe.

Green Mountains section 

  • Day-use Area and trailhead visitor facilities, including toilets
  • Rainforest return track
  • Centenary track
  • Morans Falls track 
  • Python Rock track 
  • Box Forest circuit
  • West Canungra Creek circuit
  • Toolona Creek circuit
  • Albert River circuit - Partially open from the southern junction (second turn-off) to Echo Point only - once you reach Echo Point, please return via the same track)

Remote bush camp sites (including on and off track)

  • Mount Bithongabel bush camp (on-track)
  • Echo Point bush camp (on-track)
  • Rat-a-tat remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Stinson remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Point Lookout remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Running Creek remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Lost World - Saddle remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Lost world - Creek remote bush camp (off-track)
  • Illinbah bush camp (on-track)
  • Darlington remote bush camp (off-track)

Please refer to the Binna Burra section map and Green Mountains section map for more information on locations.

All camping permits for closed areas are suspended for the duration of the closure.

Commercial tour operators and other permit or agreement holders are not permitted to enter closed areas.

Please observe all signage, barriers and directions from rangers and do not enter closed areas.

For your safety please use caution whilst visiting the park.

alerts.title: Notice of extended baiting program for feral pigs and foxes on Conway National Park, Dryander National Park, Dryander State Forest and Dryander Forest Reserve
alerts.description: The Department of the Environment, Tourism, Science and Innovation is undertaking a ground baiting program from 25 March 2026 to 25 March 2027. Manufactured baits will be used to control feral pigs and foxes on Conway National Park (Lot 43 on NPW1144 and Lot 1 on NPW1145), Dryander National Park (Lot 24 on AP19346), Dryander State Forest (Lot 432 on AP19346) and Dryander Forest Reserve (Lot 5 on AP19346). Notices will be placed at the Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service office at Jubilee Pocket and the Whitsunday Regional Council offices at Proserpine and Cannonvale for one week prior to each baiting event. These notices will remain in place for four weeks after baiting has finished. Notices will also be placed at the entrances of the Conway Circuit on Conway National Park and at Double Bay on Dryander National Park. A Park Alert will also be placed on the Department of Environment, Tourism, Science and Innovation website (www.detsi.qld.gov.au) for the same period. Dogs and other domestic animals are not allowed within protected areas. Steps must be taken to ensure domestic dogs do not gain access to the baits or poisoned animals. Neighbours should restrain dogs and other domestic animals during baiting programs. For more information, please contact the department on 13 QGOV (13 74 68).

alerts.title: Exposed sand cliff and dune landslide danger
alerts.description: Exposed sand cliffs and dunes along Teewah Beach, Rainbow Beach and Double Island Point are unstable and can collapse without warning.
alerts.details:
Exposed sand cliffs and dunes along Teewah Beach, Rainbow Beach and Double Island Point are unstable and can collapse without warning.

Falling boulders and landslides are common and can cause serious injury or death.

   • NEVER climb or dig into sand cliffs.

   • Avoid standing, sitting or parking near the base of the sand cliffs.

   • Supervise children at all times

For your safety please use caution whilst visiting the park.

alerts.title: Inskip Peninsula camping area boundary realignment and access changes following severe weather damage, combined with shoreline retrogression
alerts.description: A combination of ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred damage, storm surges, swell, and shoreline retrogression has eroded the eastern-facing beaches of Inskip Peninsula, and caused loss of sand, destabilisation of the dunes and loss of existing campground border fencing.
alerts.details:
A combination of ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred damage, storm surges, swell, and shoreline retrogression has eroded the eastern-facing beaches of Inskip Peninsula, and caused loss of sand, destabilisation of the dunes and loss of existing campground border fencing.

A team of geotechnical erosion experts was engaged to investigate the impacts of cyclone events at the Inskip Peninsula Recreation Area and to provide technical advice regarding site safety. Based on the findings and recommendations from the geotechnical assessment, the camping area boundaries will be realigned to establish a 20-metre retrogression buffer zone landward of the current treeline.

This boundary realignment is necessary due to ongoing coastal erosion, nearshore landslide risk, and damage sustained to existing infrastructure. The revised layout will enhance visitor safety by reducing exposure to unstable shoreline areas and future erosion hazards. The following changes apply:

M.V. SARAWAK:

  • Beachfront campsites and the former access track are no longer accessible, including the area known as “Millionaire’s Row”. This location, previously favoured by campers for its open access to solar, firmer surface, and beach proximity, is now closed.
  • Caravans are not suitable in the M.V. Sarawak camping area due to narrow access tracks and stands of cypress and oak trees.
  • When entering M.V. Sarawak, follow all signage and take the first left access track.
  • Do not proceed straight ahead—this route is now a dead end.

M.V. BEAGLE:

  • Beachfront campsites and the former beachfront access track are no longer accessible.
  • Caravans are not suitable in the M.V. Beagle camping area due to narrow access tracks and soft sand.
  • When entering M.V. Beagle, follow the signage and take the first left access track.
  • Do not proceed straight ahead—this route is now a dead end.

M.V. NATONE:

  • The Natone entrance track and eastern low lying camping areas are no longger accessible. Entry and exit to Natone is now through the former exit.
  • Caravans are not suitable in the M.V. Natone camping area due to narrow access tracks, soft sand and inability to turn around.
  • Camping in Natone is suitable to smaller tents and high clearance 4WD only. 

SS Dorrigo and Sarawak West remain the only Inskip camping areas suitable for caravans, requiring a high-clearance caravan towed by a high-clearance 4WD.

Observe all signage, barriers and directions from rangers, and do not enter closed areas.

Apply for a camping permit refund online at www.qld.gov.au/camping. Log into your account and cancel your booking to receive an automatic refund or request to change your booking to another camping area at Inskip. 

QPWS thanks you for your support and cooperation.

alerts.title: UPDATE #4 Seasonal closure, extended: Munga-Thirri National Park
alerts.description: For your safety, the seasonal closure at Munga-Thirri National Park has been extended to 31 August 2026.
alerts.details:
For your safety, the seasonal closure at Munga-Thirri National Park has been extended to 31 August 2026.

The seasonal closure at Munga-Thirri National Park is due to extreme summer temperates that can reach over 50 degrees Celsius. 

In 2026, heavy rainfall led to localised flooding throughout Munga-Thirri National Park. Subsequent rainfall has led to water laying across the desert making the terrain impassible.

For your safety, and to protect the natural and cultural values of Munga-Thirri National Park, the seasonal closure has been extended to 31 August 2026. This date aligns with road closure information available on Queensland Traffic. Rangers will monitor the conditions and this park alert will be updated as required.

Campers with an existing camping booking during this closure, please modify or cancel your booking. Refunds can be applied for online.

 

Stay up to date with weather forecasts, conditions and warnings.

alerts.description: As rebuilding works continue at the Lawn Hill Gorge section of Boodjamulla National Park (Aboriginal Land), visitors are advised below of the facilities that are currently open. Your safety while visiting is a top priority, please continue to check this park alert for up-to-date information.
alerts.details:
As rebuilding works continue at the Lawn Hill Gorge section of Boodjamulla National Park (Aboriginal Land), visitors are advised below of the facilities that are currently open. Your safety while visiting is a top priority, please continue to check this park alert for up-to-date information.

What visitor facilities are available?

Lawn Hill Gorge section

  • Duwadarri lookout walking track - OPEN 
  • Lawn Hill Creek - paddle to Indarri Falls and return - OPEN 
  • Lawn Hill Gorge camping area, including visitor amenities (toilets and showers). OPEN. Bookings essential: national park camping bookings

Constance Range walking track is temporarily closed for repair. 

All other walking tracks at the Lawn Hill Gorge section are under repair and are CLOSED. 

Riversleigh section

All visitor facilities at the Riversleigh section, including D site (part of the Australian Fossil Mammal Sites World Heritage Area) and Miyumba camping area will be open.

For your safety

Please note the following at the Lawn Hill Gorge section:

  • Construction zones: some areas remain active construction sites and are closed to visitors. Signs, bunting and fences mark these areas. Please follow all signs and directions from Rangers and on-site personnel and do not enter closed areas.
  • Less shade: as creek side vegetation regenerates, shade is limited onland and on water. Plan your visit and activities carefully and carry at least 2 lites of drinking water per person per day.
  • Watercraft areas: entry to Lawn Hill Creek is one way in and one way out. You must exit from the same location you entered. There is no landing at Indarri Falls.
  • Remote location: Lawn Hill Gorge is remote and Rangers may not alway be available. Visitors must be self-sufficient, responsible for your own safety and prepared for emergencies.
  • Emergency contacts: for emergencies, call Triple Zero (000). Consider carrying a satellite phone or personal locator beacon (PLB).

The Boodjamulla Cooperative Management Council (i.e. Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service and the Waanyi People) thank the local community and stakeholders for their patience and support while work continues to safely restore and rebuild flood-damaged facilities and infrastructure.

alerts.title: Natural Sand Movement and access to Double Island Point
alerts.description: Deep hole formations which may restrict access to and from Double Island Point
alerts.details:
Deep hole formations which may restrict access to and from Double Island Point

Regular sand movement within the saltwater lagoon at Double Island Point often results in the formation of deep holes. These natural changes can prevent vehicles from accessing the Double Island Point (Wide Bay side), the sandspit or travelling through to Rainbow Beach at any tide. This is a natural process within the Great Sandy National Park, where all environments are protected and natural coastal processes are allowed to occur.

Queensland Parks and Wildife Service maintains safe access to the beaches via the Freshwater Track and the Leisha Track.

Always drive the beaches within the Great Sandy National Park to the prevailing conditions. 

Drive two hours either side of low tide. If you are uncertain as to the depth or condition of any hole at any time - do not drive through it.

 

Stay up to date with weather forecasts, conditions and warnings.

Due to the changed conditions the visitor experience may be affected.

For your safety please use caution whilst visiting the park.

alerts.title: Scheduled dewatering works affecting river levels: Kareeya power station, Tully River, Tully Gorge National Park
alerts.description: From Tuesday 4 August to Friday 5 September 2026 (dates inclusive), CleanCo will undertake dewatering of the Kareeya hydro-power station.
alerts.details:
From Tuesday 4 August to Friday 5 September 2026 (dates inclusive), CleanCo will undertake dewatering of the Kareeya hydro-power station.

This involves removing water from the power station to enable inspections, maintenance and any necessary repairs in areas that are normally inaccessible.

 At the start of the dewatering, there will be a gradual decrease in the Tully River water level. For the duration of the work there will be no water released from the power station and the river level will remain low. Seasonal and natural catchment flows will continue to enter the river and once the dewatering is complete, Tully River levels will return to normal.

 During this time water levels may be affected near the following facilities:

  • Tully Gorge camping area
  • Tully Gorge day-use area
  • Cardstone weir lookout

 For more information on the dewatering works, please contact CleanCo on community@cleancoqld.com.au

alerts.title: Unexploded ordinance washing up on Southeast Queensland beaches
alerts.description: Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service (QPWS) is warning visitors of the potential dangers posed by unexploded ordinance. QPWS Rangers have reported a number of these devices washing up on Southeast Queensland beaches.
alerts.details:
Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service (QPWS) is warning visitors of the potential dangers posed by unexploded ordinance. QPWS Rangers have reported a number of these devices washing up on Southeast Queensland beaches.

Unexploded ordinance (UXO) includes ammunition and other types of explosives used by the defence forces.

The UXO come in all shapes and sizes and pose significant danger. These explosives should be treated as unstable and can become more sensitive as they age. Even small UXO items can kill or maim if handled.

If you find a suspected UXO:

  • DO NOT DISTURB OR TOUCH the item as it could explode without warning. Do not attempt to move it to a “safe location”.
  • Contact the Queensland Police on Triple Zero (000) immediately so the UXO can be reported to the Australian Defence Force for removal.
  • Provide details of the object to the 000 operator, such as:
    • approximate dimensions and general appearance
    • record the location with a GPS or phone coordinates

Keep your distance, ensure bystanders are kept well clear and follow any instructions given by the 000 operator.

Please observe all signage, barriers and directions from rangers and do not enter closed areas.

alerts.title: SEVERE EROSION  Inskip barge landing beach access tracks
alerts.description: Severe erosion has occurred at the Inskip barge landing beach access tracks due to natural coastal processes and ongoing tidal inundation.
alerts.details:
Severe erosion has occurred at the Inskip barge landing beach access tracks due to natural coastal processes and ongoing tidal inundation.

As a result of the erosion, the entrance and exit tracks have merged into a single narrow lane, requiring vehicles travelling in both directions to share the same track. These conditions are most constrained during high tide periods, when available beach width is minimal.


Visitors are strongly advised to plan travel two hours either side of low tide only, proceed with caution through the single-track section, and allow additional time for delays.


Barge operations and recommended travel times may change in response to tides and weather conditions. Visitors travelling to K’gari via the Inskip barge landing must remain up to date with Mantaray Barge advice prior to departure.


Coastal conditions are dynamic and may change rapidly. Remember:

• Check tide times – only travel 2 hours either side of low tide to K’gari.

• Only access the Inskip spit barge area when safe to do so.

• Keep barge access area clear (fishing or swimming or parking vehicles near barge access is prohibited – you will be moved on if impeding traffic. Camp in fenced areas when possible.)


QPWS thanks you for your patience and cooperation


Your safety is our concern, but your responsibility.

alerts.title: Wongari (Dingo) Whelping Season
alerts.description: Following the autumn mating season, many female dingoes (wongari) will be pregnant. Gestation lasts around 9 weeks and litters of 1  10 pups (usually 4  6) are born (whelped) and cared for during the winter months. Take particular care and slow down when driving on Kgari to avoid injuring dingoes and other wildlife. Give dingoes space, Kgari is their place.
alerts.details:
Following the autumn mating season, many female dingoes (wongari) will be pregnant. Gestation lasts around 9 weeks and litters of 1 10 pups (usually 4 6) are born (whelped) and cared for during the winter months. Take particular care and slow down when driving on Kgari to avoid injuring dingoes and other wildlife. Give dingoes space, Kgari is their place.

Dingo whelping season means:

  • Once new-born pups arrive their parents may be aggressive as they need to find food for themselves and their pups
  • Alpha females with pups are very domineering during this stage and can even kill another female’s pups if she has mated with one of the pack
  • Every time a dingo receives food from people its chance of survival is reduced – let young wongari grow up wild, do not feed them or leave food available

Visitors, tour operators and residents are advised to be dingo-safe! on K’gari. It is recommended that families with young children camp in fenced QPWS camping areas at Lake Boomanjin, Central Station, Dundubara, Waddy Point and eastern beach fenced areas (Wongai, One Tree Rocks, Cornwells, Eli), or at fenced private camping areas at Dilli Village or Cathedral Beach.

Be dingo-safe! Visit https://parks.detsi.qld.gov.au/parks/kgari-fraser/about/wongari-dingoes/dingo-safe

  • NEVER feed dingoes.
  • Always stay within arm’s reach of children, even small teenagers.
  • Walk in groups and carry a long dingo safety stick.
  • Do not run or jog outside fenced areas - this can attract a dingo’s attention and trigger their instinct to chase.
  • Camp in fenced areas when possible.
  • Secure all rubbish, fish, bait and tackle (even on boats).
  • Never store food or food containers in tents.
  • No food to lakeshores.

Report dingo encounters – email dingo.ranger@detsi.qld.gov.au, tell the nearest QPWS

ranger, or phone (07) 4127 9150 – mobile phone charges may apply. Take note of location,

vehicle registration, dingo ear tag colour, number and which ear.

For further information about wongari (dingoes) on K’gari (Fraser Island) please visit

https://parks.detsi.qld.gov.au/parks/kgari-fraser/about/wongari-dingoes

Your safety is our concern, but your responsibility.

Please visit this park's page on the department's website for more park safety information.

These works will include upgraded parking, improved viewing platforms, enhanced accessibility features to better support visitors with disabilities and new interpretive signs highlighting the areas rich cultural and natural heritage.

At certain times during this period, the following visitor facilities may be impacted by partial closures:

  • Josephine Falls car park
  • Josephine Falls toilets and day-use area
  • Josephine Falls walking track and swimming areas, including:
    • Access to bottom pool swimming area.
    • Middle creek viewing platform.
    • Top viewing platform.

See Josephine Falls map for further information on visitor facilities.

During this period, the Eastern approach, Bartle Frere trail will be temporarily closed due to management operations and access restrictions during these works. This temporary closure will include:

  • Big Rock camping area
  • Eastern Summit camping area
  • Eastern approach, Bartle Frere trail – from Josephine Falls to Bartle Frere summit, including Broken nose section.

Please monitor park alerts for the most up-to-date information on access and temporary closures.

alerts.title: Curtis Island , Grahmams Creek Road, Ship Hill Day Use Area, Multi Use Trails 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 Closed
alerts.description: The Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service (QPWS) conducts planned burns on parks and forests to support a healthy environment and reduce the risk and intensity of bushfires. QPWS will conduct a planned burn in Curtis Island Environmental Management Precinct between the 20th and 25th of JULY 2026 As a result of the planned burn, smoke may be seen in nearby areas including Gladstone, Rockhampton, Emu Park, Rosslyn Bay and Yeppoon and the community of Southend. If visiting Curtis Island National Park observe all signs, barriers, and directions from QPWS Rangers and never enter closed areas.
alerts.details:
The Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service (QPWS) conducts planned burns on parks and forests to support a healthy environment and reduce the risk and intensity of bushfires. QPWS will conduct a planned burn in Curtis Island Environmental Management Precinct between the 20th and 25th of JULY 2026 As a result of the planned burn, smoke may be seen in nearby areas including Gladstone, Rockhampton, Emu Park, Rosslyn Bay and Yeppoon and the community of Southend. If visiting Curtis Island National Park observe all signs, barriers, and directions from QPWS Rangers and never enter closed areas.

Avoid driving through smoke where possible and be aware of firefighters working in the
area.
Planned burns are vital in promoting ecological health, biodiversity, and the conservation of
species and habitats.
The aim of this burn is to maintain a healthy forest environment and reduce the potential
threats from bushfires in periods of dangerous fire weather.
Fire is important in many of Queensland’s natural ecosystems to maintain the diversity of
native plants and animals. Fire plays a vital role in maintaining these ecosystems through
releasing nutrients, encouraging seed germination and providing ideal conditions for
growth.
Only a portion of the landscape is burnt at any one time. This helps to create a mosaic
pattern of burnt and unburnt areas that increases the opportunity for safer and more
successful response to bushfires while supporting plant regeneration and protecting
important habitat for wildlife.
Public safety is the highest consideration in all QPWS fire management activities. To find out
more visit the Fire management webpage.
Live air quality data, and information on smoke and dust health actions, can be found on the
Queensland Government’s website:
https://www.qld.gov.au/environment/management/monitoring/air.

You can stay updated with local QPWS fire activities by visiting Park Alerts or by following
Qld Park Alerts on X (formerly known as Twitter).
For more information, please call Patrick Centurino, Senior Ranger Fire, on 0408 764 158.

Stay up-to-date with Current bushfire warnings and incidents from Queensland Fire Department (QFD).

alerts.title: Challenging seasonal hiking conditions
alerts.description: Conditions are extremely hot and humid at this time of year, increasing the risk of heat-related injuries. Hikers can suffer heat exhaustion.
alerts.details:
Conditions are extremely hot and humid at this time of year, increasing the risk of heat-related injuries. Hikers can suffer heat exhaustion.

Freshwater sources often dry up in October-December. The wet season usually begins in December. When the wet season starts, high rainfall can cause creeks to rise rapidly and crossings to become dangerous. Extreme weather events are also more likely during the wet season.

QPWS strongly recommends careful consideration of choosing to undertake the Thorsborne Trail hike during the summer months.     

The Thorsborne Trail is closed seasonally from the start of January to the end of February due to the high risk of unsafe conditions over this period.

If visiting this section of Noosa National Park, observe all signs, barriers, and directions from QPWS Rangers and never enter closed areas.

Planned burns are vital in promoting ecological health, biodiversity, and the conservation of species and habitats.

They also aim to mitigate the risk and intensity of bushfires and their potential impacts on communities, infrastructure, parks, and forests.

Planned burns are highly weather-dependent, generally requiring milder conditions and minimal wind.

These same weather conditions can also allow smoke to accumulate and linger until weather conditions change and winds assist in dispersing the smoke.

If smoke affects you:

  • stay indoors and close windows and doors;
  • keep respiratory medication close by.

Public safety is the highest consideration in all QPWS fire management activities. To find out more visit the Fire management webpage.

Please monitor park alerts on the department's website for updated information.

Stay up-to-date with Current bushfire warnings and incidents from Queensland Fire Department (QFD).

alerts.details:
Visitors are advised that Josephine Creek within Josephine Falls section, Wooroonooran National Park is closed until further notice.

For your safety, do not enter the water. The creek is closed.

  • Heavy rain has resulted in fast flowing, high water levels in Josephine Creek.
  • Further rain is expected.
  • Water levels can change rapidly in Josephine Creek.

The walking track and viewing platforms remain open and can still be accessed.

alerts.title: Temporary walking track closure: Rainforest circuit track and Rex Creek swing bridge, Mossman Gorge, Daintree National Park (CYPAL)

alerts.details:
The Rainforest circuit track and Rex Creek swing bridge at Mossman Gorge in Daintree National Park (CYPAL) are closed until further notice.

This closure is due to vandalism resulting in damage to the Rex Creek swing bridge, that now requires emergency repairs.

This closure includes:

  • Rainforest circuit track
  • Manjal Dimbi (Mount Demi) lookout
  • Rex Creek swing bridge.

For visitor safety, access across the bridge is not permitted while repairs are being arranged and completed.

The bridge will remain closed until repairs are completed and the structure has been assessed as safe to reopen.

Visitors are asked to obey all signage and barriers in place.

Please monitor park alerts on the department's website for updated information.
